summary: This post introduces an analogy between the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern from web development and modern data architecture's semantic layer. Späti argues that just as MVC separates presentation from business logic through a model layer, data teams need a similar separation between data storage and presentation through semantic layers. The post serves as an introduction to a longer article published on Cube.dev, positioning semantic layers as the data world's equivalent to Active Record in Ruby on Rails. The core thesis is that proven software architecture patterns can solve similar challenges in data engineering around metric definition and maintenance.
key_points: MVC's separation of concerns principle from the late 70s has direct parallels to modern data architecture challenges
Semantic layers serve as the 'model' equivalent in data, providing a declarative place to define metrics, measures, and business KPIs
Active Record patterns from Ruby on Rails map conceptually to how semantic layers work in data modeling
This architectural approach enables confident updates and versioning of business logic
The pattern addresses metric definition and maintenance challenges that plague data teams
money_quotes: Wouldn't it be convenient to separate the presentation from the storage through a data modeling layer, similar to the model layer?
A place where we can declaratively define our metrics, measures, and business KPIs so that we can update them confidently and version them.
Today, we have the beginning of such a separation of concerns with a Semantic Layer.
This architectural pattern can solve similar challenges in data engineering that MVC solved for web development.
key_insight: The MVC pattern's separation of concerns, proven over decades in web development, provides a useful mental model for understanding why semantic layers matter in modern data architecture.
